summary: Dr. Mark Tomforde describes CHAMP, a math outreach program at the University of Houston that serves predominantly Black and Hispanic students from Third Ward and Sunnyside—among America's most dangerous and impoverished neighborhoods. The program brings high school students to campus twice weekly for Math Circle-style lessons and SAT/ACT tutoring, while connecting them with college student mentors who share similar backgrounds. Tomforde argues that mathematics is uniquely positioned as an equalizer because math skills are critical gatekeepers for high school graduation, college admission, and STEM success. He emphasizes that mathematical ability is equally distributed across populations, but opportunity is not—meaning countless potential Einsteins are lost to poverty and under-resourced schools. The piece calls for universities to break out of their bubbles and engage surrounding communities, framing this not as charity but as central to their educational mission.
key_points: CHAMP serves students from neighborhoods where 1 in 11 people are victims of violent crime annually, bringing them to a university campus for math enrichment and mentorship
Mathematics functions as a critical gatekeeper: it's often the biggest obstacle to high school graduation and a primary factor in college admissions and STEM success
Exposing students to college mentors who look like them and share their backgrounds addresses stereotype threat and provides crucial role models
Mathematical ability is equally distributed across populations, but opportunity is not—creating what Tomforde calls 'one of the greatest tragedies of the modern age'
Homogeneity in STEM is self-defeating: diverse perspectives and unconventional thinking are essential for actual problem-solving
money_quotes: Math ability is spread pretty much equally through the population, and we see there are almost no low-income, high-performing math students.

What if the next Einstein or the next Steve Jobs or the person capable of curing cancer is born in poverty and attends an under-resourced school in inner city America? Their potential contributions will most likely be lost.

We have created a system in which only a homogenous group of individuals ever have the chance to work on these problems. If we are actually interested in solving our problems, this just doesn't make sense.
This involvement in the local community is not ancillary to a university's educational mission, but rather central to it.
Too often universities exist within a bubble, disconnected from their surrounding community.

Quality mathematics education can improve high school graduation rates, help students get into a better college with more support, and improve the chances of success for students majoring in STEM fields.
key_insight: Mathematical ability is equally distributed across all populations, but access to mathematical opportunity is not—making math education both a symptom of inequality and a uniquely powerful lever for addressing it.
